lesson:
問題もんだいない — two words, total reassurance. "No problem." You'll hear it as a response to apologies, worries, and questions about whether something went wrong. Its slightly cockier variant 問題もんだいなし sounds like snapping a case shut: done, settled, nothing to discuss.

But 問題もんだい itself isn't always about problems. It covers "question" (on a test: 問題もんだいを解とく solve a question), "issue" (in society: 社会あしゃかい問題もんだい), and "matter" (問題もんだいは時間まじかんがないことだ the matter is that there's no time). The word is less alarming than English "problem" — it's closer to "the thing on the table that needs attention." A teacher handing out an exam says 問題もんだい without implying anything has gone wrong.

The kanji combination is poetic: 問もん shows a mouth (口くち) at a gate (門もん) — someone calling out a question. 題だい means topic. A question raised as a topic — that's a 問題もんだい.

必ひつ (certainly, necessarily) + 要よう (need, essential point). What is certainly needed. パスポートが必要ひつようです (a passport is necessary). 必要ひつような書か類 (necessary documents). It works as a な-adjective (必要ひつような) and as a noun (必要ひつようがある — there is a need).

The kanji 必ひつ is worth examining. It looks like a heart (心こころ) pierced by a line — and some etymologists read it that way: something that pierces straight to the heart, unavoidable, certain. Others see it as a stake driven into the ground — something fixed, immovable. Either way, it carries absolute certainty. 必ひつず (かならず, without fail) uses the same kanji.

Opposite: 不必要ひつよう (ふひつよう, unnecessary) — but in conversation people more often say 必要ひつようない or いらない (don't need it).

Dictionaryひつようだ
Negativeひつようじゃない
Pastひつようだった
Past negひつようじゃなかった
Adverbひつように
て-formひつようで

This is not 持もつ (to hold, possess) — though they sound identical. This もつ means to last, to endure, to hold up over time. この電池でんちはよくもつ (this battery lasts well). このパンは三日みっかはもつ (this bread will keep for three days). 体からだがもたない (my body can't take it anymore).

It's usually written in hiragana precisely to avoid confusion with 持もつ. When you see もつ in hiragana followed by a duration or without a direct object, it's this word — the durability one.

The negative もたない is especially common and expressive. あと十じゅう分わももたない (it won't even last ten more minutes). It conveys that something is at its limit — fuel running out, patience wearing thin, food going bad.

Particles: もつ takes が — the thing that lasts is the subject. 電でん池がもつ (the battery lasts). No direct object.

Passive: Rare — you wouldn't naturally put durability into passive voice.

Causative を／にもたせる: to make something last. 冷つめ蔵庫に入はいれてもたせる (put it in the fridge to make it last).

五段 Godan自動詞
Dictionaryもつが
Negativeもたないが
Politeもちますが
Polite negもちませんが
Pastもったが
Polite pastもちましたが
て-formもってが
Volitionalもとうが
Polite volもちましょうが
Passive—
Causative—

Cond ～たらもったらが

You already know 開あける (あける) — to open something (transitive). 開あく read as あく is the intransitive partner: the thing opens by itself. ドアが開あいた (the door opened). 店みせが開あいている (the store is open).

The reading trap: 開あく has two intransitive readings. As あく, it's everyday opening — doors, stores, eyes, mouths. As ひらく, it's more abstract or ceremonial — flowers blooming, events being held, possibilities opening up. ドアがあく (the door opens). 花はながひらく (a flower opens/blooms). Context and furigana are your only guides in written text.

The pair 開あく/閉しまる (open/close, both intransitive) matches the transitive pair 開あける/閉しめる. Lock these four together in your mind and door-related Japanese becomes effortless.

Particles: 開あく (あく) takes が — the thing that opens is the subject. 窓まどが開あいている (the window is open).

Passive: Rare for intransitive verbs. Use the plain form.

Causative を／に開あかせる: making something open. Usually you'd just use 開あける instead.

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 開ける (あける)

昨年さくねん and 去年きょねん (きょねん) both mean "last year," but they live in different registers. 去年きょねん is everyday conversation — what you'd say to a friend. 昨年さくねん is what you'd write in a business email, hear in a news broadcast, or read in a formal report. Same meaning, different clothing.

昨さく means "yesterday/previous" and shows up in a family of formal time words: 昨日きのう (さくじつ, yesterday — formal; the casual reading is きのう), 昨夜よるさくや (さくや, last night — formal), 昨年としさくねん (さくねん, last year). The さく reading always signals formality.

When in doubt, use 去年きょねん in conversation and 昨年さくねん in writing. Mixing them up won't cause a misunderstanding, but using 昨年さくねん in casual chat sounds stiff, like saying "the preceding calendar year" when you just mean "last year."

meaning: to get healed, be cured, get well
lesson:
You know 直なおる (なおる) — to get fixed, repaired (machines, objects). 治なおる is the same sound, same conjugation, but for the body: to heal, to recover from illness. 風邪かぜが治なおった (my cold got better). 傷きずが治なおる (the wound heals).

The kanji 治ち has the water radical (氵さんずい) plus 台だい — originally the character depicted controlling or managing water, as in irrigation and flood control. From "controlling water" it drifted to "governing" (政治せいじ, せいじ, politics) and then to "curing" — restoring order to a disordered body. The metaphor: disease is chaos in the body, and healing is bringing it under control.

Both 治なおる and 直なおる are intransitive — the healing or fixing happens on its own. Their transitive partners: 治なおす (なおす, to cure) and 直なおす (なおす, to fix).

Particles: 治なおる takes が — the illness or injury is the subject. 病気びょうきが治なおる (the illness gets better).

Passive: Rare — illness healing is already a spontaneous process.

Causative を／に治なおらせる: making something heal. Uncommon — you'd use 治なおす instead.

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 治す (なおす)

Negativeなおらないが
Politeなおりますが
Polite negなおりませんが
Pastなおったが
Polite pastなおりましたが
て-formなおってが
Volitionalなおろうが
Polite volなおりましょうが

Causative—

Cond ～たらなおったらが

In Japan, prices are in 円えん. But the moment a conversation turns to foreign goods, travel budgets, or exchange rates, ドル enters. 今いま、一ドル何円なんえん？ (what's the dollar at now?) is a question any internationally-minded Japanese person might ask daily.

There's a cultural layer here. For decades, the ドル/円 exchange rate has been a bellwether of Japan's economic health, making front-page news. 円高えんだか (えんだか, strong yen) and 円安えんやす (えんやす, weak yen) are defined against the dollar. When Japanese news says ドルが上うえがった (the dollar went up), everyone understands the economic implications.

Walk into a Japanese office and someone will eventually sigh, システムが落おちた (the system crashed). In business Japanese, システム almost always means IT — the network, the software, the infrastructure. This is where the katakana word lives: technology and modernity.

Japanese has its own words for "system" — 制度せいど (せいど, institutional system — tax system, education system), 仕組しくみ (しくみ, mechanism — how something works), 体制たいせい (たいせい, regime/structure). But none of these cover what happens when you plug in a computer. システム filled that gap and now dominates: システムエンジニア (systems engineer), システム開あ発 (system development), システム障害 (system failure). If you work in tech in Japan, this word will follow you home.

meaning: more than or equal to, that's all
lesson:
以上いじょう has two very different uses that only context distinguishes.

Quantity: "X or more." 十人じゅうにん以上いじょう (ten or more people). 三十度さんじゅうど以上いじょう (30 degrees or higher). Crucially, 以上いじょう includes the number it attaches to. "Ten or more" means ten counts. This is different from English "more than ten" (which excludes ten). The distinction matters on exams and in legal contexts.

Conclusion: "That is all." 以い上うえです at the end of a presentation or announcement means "that concludes my remarks." 報告は以い上うえです (that's the end of the report). It's crisp, professional, final — the verbal equivalent of closing a folder.

The pair: 以上いじょう (≥, above) and 以下いか (いか, ≤, below). 十じゅう八はち歳以上うえ (18 and older). 十じゅう二に歳以下した (12 and younger). Both include the boundary number.

最近さいきんどう？ (how've you been lately?) — this is how catch-up conversations start in Japanese. 最近さいきん is the first word out of the gate, and it covers an elastic span of time. Yesterday? Last month? This whole season? All valid. The word has no fixed clock — it just means "the recent past that feels relevant right now."

最近さいきん、髪かみ切きった？ (did you get a haircut recently?) could refer to yesterday or two weeks ago. 最近ちかさいきんの若わか者は (young people these days...) stretches "recently" to mean the entire current generation. The vagueness is a feature: 最近さいきん lets you reference a period without committing to exactly how long ago it was.

Grammar-wise, it pulls double duty as noun and adverb with no particle needed. 最近ちかさいきん読よんだ本ほん (a book I recently read). 最近ちかさいきんの天気てんき (the recent weather). The kanji — 最さい (most) + 近きん (near) — literally mean "the nearest." Nearest to now.

世界せかい started as a Buddhist term — it meant the entire universe of sentient beings, the bounded realm we all share. That origin still echoes in how the word is used. 世界せかい isn't just the physical globe; it's any complete, self-contained realm. 音楽おんがくの世界せかい (the world of music). 夢の世界せかい (the world of dreams). Each one has its own rules, its own inhabitants.

Two compounds you'll see constantly: 世界中せかいじゅう (せかいじゅう, all over the world) and 世界一せかいいち (せかいいち, the best in the world). That 〜中なか pattern — adding じゅう to a place word to mean "throughout" — works everywhere: 日にち本ほん中なか, 町まち中なか, 学校がっこう中なか. And 世界せかい一 carries real weight — when Japanese media calls someone 世界せかい一, it's a superlative that demands to be taken literally.

The full English word "computer," reshaped by Japanese phonology. Every consonant gets a vowel, every cluster gets broken apart: com-pyu-u-ta-a. The long ー marks are stretching the vowels to approximate the English rhythm.

In practice, almost nobody says コンピューター in casual conversation. They say パソコン (from "personal computer") for a desktop or laptop, or スマホ (from "smartphone") for a phone. コンピューター shows up more in technical, formal, or general contexts — コンピューターサイエンス (computer science), コンピューターウイルス (computer virus). It's the category word; パソコン is what people actually use at their desks.

There's a stylistic choice in how to spell it: コンピューター (with the final ー) vs. コンピュータ (without it). Technical writing sometimes drops the trailing ー; everyday usage keeps it. Both are correct.

This is the second time やる appears in the Core 1000 — word 172 was the first. That repetition tells you how common and how versatile this verb is. It means "to do" but with a hands-on, action-oriented edge that する doesn't have.

する is neutral: 勉強べんきょうする (study). やる is punchy: やるしかない (there's nothing to do but do it). やってみる (give it a try). やった！ (I did it! / Yes!). Where する is a button press, やる is rolling up your sleeves.

The "give" meaning is separate and old-fashioned: 花はなに水みずをやる (water the flowers — give water to the flowers). 犬いぬにえさをやる (feed the dog). This "giving" only goes to inferiors, animals, or plants — never to equals or superiors. For people, use あげる instead.

Particles: やる takes を for the thing you do: 仕事しごとをやる. For the "give" meaning, に marks the recipient and を marks the thing given: 猫ねこにえさをやる.

Potential がやれる: が — 俺にもやれる (even I can do it).

Passive にやられる: に marks the agent. 敵てきにやられた (I got beaten by the enemy). やられる often means being done in or defeated.

Causative に…をやらせる: に marks who you make do it. 後あと輩にやらせる (make the junior do it).

五段 Godan他動詞
Dictionaryやるを
Negativeやらないを
Politeやりますを
Polite negやりませんを
Pastやったを
Polite pastやりましたを
て-formやってを
Volitionalやろうを
Polite volやりましょうを
Passiveやられるに
Causativeやらせるに／を
Caus-passやらせられるに
Potentialやれるが
Imperativeやれを
Imp なさいやりなさいを
Cond ～ばやればを
Cond ～たらやったらを

どういう意味いみ？ (what does that mean?) — you will say this sentence more than almost any other while learning Japanese. It's the universal "explain that to me" request. Memorize it first, understand its components later.

The kanji tell an interesting story: 意い (mind) + 味み (flavor). The flavor of a thought. Japanese treats meaning as something you taste — you roll a word around in your mind the way you'd roll food around in your mouth. The metaphor works in reverse too: 味あじがある (it has flavor) can mean something is meaningful or interesting.

意味いみがない goes beyond "meaningless" — it means "pointless, not worth doing." もう遅おそいから行いっても意味いみがない (it's already late, so going would be pointless). When something has no 意味いみ, it has no purpose, no value, no reason to exist. The word holds both definition and justification.

増ふえる is intransitive — things increase on their own. Population grows, work piles up, weight creeps on. 仕事しごとが増ふえた (work has increased). 体重たいじゅうが増ふえている (I've been gaining weight). The transitive partner is 増ふやす (ふやす) — to increase something deliberately.

The kanji 増ぞう has the earth radical (土つち) and a component meaning "layers." Earth piling up in layers — things accumulating, growing. It's the kanji of gradual accumulation rather than sudden explosion.

増ふえる pairs naturally with 減へる (へる, to decrease). Learn them together: 人ひと口くちが増ふえる / 人ひと口くちが減へる. 給料りょうきゅうりょうが増ふえる / 給料りょうきゅうりょうが減へる. They're two sides of every graph.

Particles: 増ふえる takes が — the thing increasing is the subject. 人ひと口くちが増ふえる (the population increases).

Passive: Already intransitive, so passive is unnatural. Use the plain form.

Causative を／に増ふえさせる: uncommon — use 増ふやす instead when you want to express making something increase.

一段 Ichidan自動詞 — pair: 増やす (ふやす)

Negativeふえないが
Politeふえますが
Polite negふえませんが
Pastふえたが

て-formふえてが

The kanji 選せん has the movement radical (辶しんにょう) and a complex right side that originally depicted two people being sorted — the act of selection, of separating the chosen from the rest. The same kanji appears in 選手せんしゅ (せんしゅ, athlete — "selected player") and 選挙せんきょ (せんきょ, election — "selecting and raising up").

えらぶ covers everything from picking a restaurant to electing a president. 好すきなのを選えらんで (pick whichever one you like). 言葉ことばを選えらぶ (choose one's words carefully). That second example is interesting — it implies deliberation, care, filtering. When you 選えらぶ, you're considering options and making a judgment.

選ぶ vs 決める: 選えらぶ is choosing from options. 決きめる (きめる) is deciding, committing. You 選えらぶ first, then 決きめる. レストランを選えらんで、メニューを決きめる (choose a restaurant, then decide what to order).

Particles: 選えらぶ takes を — the thing chosen is the direct object. プレゼントを選えらぶ (choose a present). Use から for what you're choosing from: 三さんつの中なかから選えらぶ (choose from three).

Potential が選えらべる: が — what you can choose becomes the subject. 色いろが選えらべる (you can choose the color).

Passive に選えらばれる: に marks who does the choosing. キャプテンに選えらばれた (I was chosen as captain).

Causative に…を選えらばせる: に marks who you let choose. 子供こどもに好すきなおもちゃを選えらばせる (let the kid choose their favorite toy).

五段 Godan他動詞

Polite pastえらびましたを
て-formえらんでを

Causativeえらばせるに／を
Caus-passえらばせられるに

Imperativeえらべを
Imp なさいえらびなさいを

Cond ～たらえらんだらを

Japanese has two words for "life" and using the wrong one sounds strange. 人生じんせい (じんせい) is your whole life story — birth to death, the grand arc. 生活せいかつ is Tuesday. It's the daily grind: what you eat, how you commute, whether you can pay rent. 生活費せいかつひ (せいかつひ, living expenses). 生活せいかつが苦にがしい (daily life is tough — financially). 生活せいかつのために働はたらく (work to make a living).

生活せいかつする as a verb means "to live" with emphasis on the everyday experience: 日にち本ほんで生活せいかつする (live in Japan). Compare with 住すむ, which just means to reside. You 住すむ at an address. You 生活せいかつする in a routine — groceries, trains, seasons, neighbors. The word holds the full texture of being somewhere, not just the coordinates.

進すすめる is the transitive "advance" — you push something forward. A plan, a project, a conversation, a clock hand. 計画けいかくを進すすめる (advance the plan). 話はなしを進すすめる (move the conversation forward). 時とき計を五分わ進すすめる (set the clock five minutes ahead).

Its intransitive partner 進すすむ (すすむ) means things advance on their own — work progresses, time moves forward, you walk ahead. 進すすめる adds your hand on the wheel: you're the one making it happen.

進める vs 勧める: Both are read すすめる. 進すすめる = move something forward. 勧める = recommend something. Completely different meanings, same sound. Written Japanese distinguishes them; spoken Japanese relies on context.

Particles: 進すすめる takes を — the thing you advance is your direct object. プロジェクトを進すすめる (advance the project).

Potential が進すすめられる: が — 計画けいかくが進すすめられる (the plan can be advanced).

Passive に進すすめられる: に marks who advances it. 議ぎ長ながに話はなを進すすめられた (the chairman moved the discussion forward).

Causative に…を進すすめさせる: に marks who you make advance something. 部へ下したに作つく業を進すすめさせる (have the subordinate move the work forward).

一段 Ichidan他動詞 — pair: 進む (すすむ)

続つづける is the transitive "continue" — you keep doing something by choice. 勉強べんきょうを続つづける (continue studying). 走はしり続つづける (keep running). Its intransitive partner 続つづく (つづく) means something continues on its own — a road extends, rain persists, a story goes on.

The most important pattern: verb stem + 続ける. Attach 続つづける to the masu-stem of any verb and it means "keep doing that verb." 食たべ続つづける (keep eating), 話はなし続つづける (keep talking), 信じ続つづける (keep believing). This is one of the most productive compound-verb patterns in Japanese. Once you learn it, you can express sustained action with any verb in the language.

The kanji 続ぞく has the thread radical (糸いと) — threads being drawn out continuously, one after another. An unbroken filament. That image of an unspooling thread is exactly what 続つづける feels like: pulling the action forward without letting it snap.

Particles: 続つづける takes を — the thing you continue is the direct object. 仕事しごとを続つづける (continue working).

Potential が続つづけられる: が — この生活せいかつが続つづけられない (I can't keep living like this).

Passive に続つづけられる: に marks the agent. Uncommon in practice.

Causative に…を続つづけさせる: に marks who you make continue. 生う徒に練習れんしゅうを続つづけさせる (make students keep practicing).

一段 Ichidan他動詞 — pair: 続く (つづく)

ほとんど flips meaning based on the sentence's polarity, and getting this wrong will say the opposite of what you mean.

With negative verbs: "hardly, barely, almost not." ほとんど寝ねていない (I've hardly slept). ほとんど食たべなかった (I barely ate). This is the more common usage.

With positive verbs: "almost, mostly, nearly all." 宿題しゅくだいはほとんど終おわった (homework is almost done). ほとんどの人ひとが賛成さんせいした (most people agreed).

The pattern is the same in both cases: ほとんど means "approaching the limit." In a positive sentence, it approaches completion. In a negative sentence, it approaches zero. The polarity of the verb determines which direction the approach goes.

No kanji in standard use — this is almost always written in hiragana. The kanji 殆ほとんど exists but is uncommon outside formal writing.

会かい (meeting) + 社しゃ (shrine, society). A place where people gather. But 社しゃ is the interesting piece: its left radical is 示じ, the altar radical, and 土つち on the right is earth. A sacred mound of earth — a shrine to the local land god. In ancient Japan, the 社しゃ was where a community came together. That's why "company" and "shrine" share a kanji: both are places that bind people into a collective.

In Japan, 会社かいしゃ isn't just where you work. It's closer to a social identity. 会社員かいしゃいん (かいしゃいん, company employee) is a standard answer to "what do you do?" — not your specific role, just the fact that you belong to a company. 会社かいしゃを辞める (quit a company) carries weight that "I quit my job" doesn't in English. You're leaving the group.

会社 vs 企業: 企業ぎょう (きぎょう) also means company, but it's formal and refers to the business as an economic entity — the kind of word you'd see in financial news. 会社かいしゃ is the word people actually use when talking about where they work. うちの会社かいしゃ (my company — notice the うち, marking it as in-group).

You've already met this kanji as うち — the warm, tribal word for home, your in-group. いえ is the same character wearing different clothes. Where うち means "my place, my people," いえ means the physical structure. Walls, roof, rooms. 新あたらしい家いえを建たてる (build a new house). 家いえが大おおきい (the house is big). You can point at an いえ. You belong to an うち.

But いえ has a second life that うち doesn't: lineage. 家いえを継つぐ (inherit the family line). いい家いえの出で (from a good family). In this sense, いえ is the household stretching across generations — the family as an institution. This is the same kanji that produces 家族かぞく (family), 家いえ庭 (household), and 家いえ as a suffix meaning "-ist" or "expert": 作つく家いえ (さっか, writer), 画が家いえ (がか, painter), 専門もん家いえ (せんもんか, specialist).

meaning: many, much, a lot, a large amount
lesson:
This is 多おおい in disguise. Remember how 多おおい can't go directly before a noun — you can't say 多い人? This is the workaround. 多おおく is the adverbial/noun form, and it behaves completely differently from the adjective it came from.

Two patterns to lock in. First, 多くの + noun: 多おおくの人ひと (many people), 多おおくの場合ばあい (in many cases). This is the standard way to say "many [things]" that 多おおい can't do on its own. Second, 多く as a standalone noun meaning "the majority": 多おおくは学生うだ (most of them are students).

The shift from い-adjective to く-form is productive across the language — 近ちかい → 近ちかく (nearby), 遠とおい → 遠とおく (far away) — but 多おおく is the one you'll use most, precisely because 多おおい has that grammatical hole it needs to fill.

話はなす (to speak) minus the verb ending gives you the noun: 話はなし, the thing that was spoken. But where English needs three different words — conversation, story, talk — Japanese uses just this one. いい話はなし could be "a good story," "a nice conversation," or "good news" depending on context. 話はなしがある (I have something to tell you) — and the tone of voice tells you whether it's exciting or ominous.

The kanji 話はなし is 言い (words, speech) plus 舌 (tongue). Words coming off the tongue. Straightforward, and that's fitting — 話はなし is one of those bedrock nouns that shows up constantly because Japanese conversation talks about talking all the time. 話はなしを聞きく (listen to what someone says). 話はなが長ながい (the story is long / they talk too much). 話はなにならない (it doesn't even count as a conversation — it's hopeless).

Heads up: 話はなし read as はなし is the noun. In compounds, the reading shifts to わ: 電話でんわ (でんわ, telephone), 会話かいわ (かいわ, conversation). Same kanji, two readings depending on whether it's standing alone or inside a compound.

上あがる is the intransitive partner of 上あげる (to raise something). Things 上あがる on their own: prices rise, temperatures climb, rain stops, someone gets nervous. You 上あげる things deliberately: you raise your hand, lift a box, turn up the volume. This pair — 上あがる / 上あげる — is one of the most common 自動詞/他動詞 splits in the language.

What makes 上あがる interesting is how far it stretches beyond literal rising. 値段ねだんが上あがる (prices go up) is predictable. 雨あめが上うえがる (the rain stops) is less obvious — the rain "rises" off the ground, clearing away. 緊張ちょうで上うえがる (get nervous / freeze up from tension) — your blood pressure rises, your composure floats away. 家いえに上あがる (step up into a house) — Japanese houses have a raised floor, so entering is literally stepping up.

That last usage connects to a cultural rule: in Japan, you remove shoes at the 玄関かん (entryway) before 上あがる-ing into the house. The physical step up marks the boundary between outside and inside. どうぞお上うえがりください (please come in) is the polite invitation.

Particles: 上あがる is intransitive — things rise on their own. The thing rising takes が: 温おん度どが上うえがる. When entering a space (stepping up), it takes に: 家いえに上あがる.

Passive に上うえがられる: indirect passive — someone's rising affects you. 値段に上うえがられると困こまる (if prices rise on me, I'm in trouble). に marks the thing whose rising impacts you.

Causative を／に上うえがらせる: making or letting something/someone rise. を leans coercive, に leans permissive. お客きゃくさんを部へ屋やに上うえがらせる (have the guest come up into the room).

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 上げる (あげる)
Dictionaryあがるが
Negativeあがらないが
Politeあがりますが
Polite negあがりませんが
Pastあがったが
Polite pastあがりましたが
て-formあがってが
Volitionalあがろうが
Polite volあがりましょうが
Passive—
Causative—
Caus-pass—

Imperativeあがれが
Imp なさいあがりなさいが
Cond ～ばあがればが
Cond ～たらあがったらが

meaning: now, soon; already (past), anymore (neg); another, again
lesson:
Three words wearing one disguise. もう has three distinct meanings, and context is the only way to tell them apart:

もう + past = "already." もう食たべた (I already ate). The action happened sooner than expected. もう + negative = "not anymore." もう食たべない (I won't eat anymore / I'm done eating). Something that was true has stopped. もう + quantity = "another, one more." もう一ひとつ (one more). もう少すくし (a little more).

The thread connecting all three: もう always signals a shift from the current state. Something has already shifted (past). Something won't shift back (negative). Something needs to shift further (quantity). It's a word about thresholds being crossed.

もう vs まだ: These two form a natural pair. もう means the change happened. まだ means it hasn't. もう来くた？ (did they come already?) / まだ来くていない (they haven't come yet). Keep them as a pair and they calibrate each other.

The kanji 集しゅう is a bird (隹ふるとり) landing on a tree (木き). Not one bird — the idea is many birds converging on one tree. A flock gathering. That image is exactly what 集あつめる means: you actively bring scattered things to one place.

集あつめる is transitive — you do the collecting. Its intransitive partner is 集あつまる (あつまる) — things gather on their own. データを集あつめる (collect data — you go get it). 人ひとが集あつまる (people gather — they come by themselves). The める/まる pair is one of Japanese's most productive: the める version means you cause it, the まる version means it happens naturally.

What can you 集あつめる? Almost anything. 切手きってを集あつめる (collect stamps). 注ちゅう目めを集あつめる (attract attention — literally, collect people's gazes). 情報じょうほうを集あつめる (gather information). 勇気きを集あつめる (gather courage). The word works for physical objects and abstract things alike.

Particles: 集あつめる takes を — whatever you're collecting is your direct object. 情報じょうほうを集あつめる. You can add から for the source: みんなからお金かねを集あつめる (collect money from everyone).

Potential が集あつめられる: が — what can be collected becomes the subject. 十じゅう分わなデータが集あつめられる (enough data can be collected).

Passive に集あつめられる: に marks who does the collecting. 先生うせんせいに教室きょうしつに集あつめられた (we were gathered by the teacher into the classroom).

Causative に…を集あつめさせる: に marks who you make collect, を marks what they collect. 部へ下したにデータを集あつめさせる (have a subordinate collect data).

一段 Ichidan他動詞 — pair: 集まる (あつまる)
Dictionaryあつめるを
Negativeあつめないを
Politeあつめますを
Polite negあつめませんを
Pastあつめたを
Polite pastあつめましたを
て-formあつめてを
Volitionalあつめようを
Polite volあつめましょうを
Passiveあつめられるに
Causativeあつめさせるに／を
Caus-passあつめさせられるに
Potentialあつめられるが
Imperativeあつめろを
Imp なさいあつめなさいを
Cond ～ばあつめればを
Cond ～たらあつめたらを

声こえ is specifically the sound that comes out of a living throat. Dogs bark, birds sing, people talk — all 声こえ. A car horn? That's 音おと (おと, sound). The dividing line: if it came from a creature's body, it's 声こえ. If it's mechanical or environmental, it's 音おと.

The word stretches into metaphor though. 国くに民の声こえ (the voice of the people — public opinion). 声こえを上うえげる (raise one's voice — speak up, protest). 声こえをかける (call out to someone — not literally about volume, just initiating contact). That last one is extremely common in daily Japanese: 分わからないことがあったら声こえをかけてください (if there's anything you don't understand, just ask).

The kanji's traditional form 聲 was far more complex — it included 耳みみ (ear) and 殳 (a hand striking), suggesting a sound that strikes the ear. The simplified modern form 声こえ lost most of those pieces, but the word kept all its resonance.

初はじめて and 始はじめて are both read はじめて and both involve beginnings — but they're different words. 初はじめて (with the "first" kanji 初しょ) means "for the first time" — an experience that's never happened before. 始はじめて (with the "start" kanji 始はじ) is just the て-form of 始はじめる (to begin). The one you want here is 初しょ: the first encounter.

初しょ is 衤 (clothing radical) + 刀かたな (blade). A blade cutting cloth — the first cut into new fabric. The moment something begins for the very first time.

初はじめて日本にほんに行いった (I went to Japan for the first time). 初めてはじめて聞きいた (first time I've heard that). The word sits naturally right before the verb it modifies. There's a common pattern worth noting: 〜て初めて — "only after doing X did I realize Y." 日にち本ほんに住すんで初めて分わかった (I only understood after actually living in Japan). The experience had to happen before the understanding could arrive.

Three verbs read かわる exist, each with a different kanji and a different shade of change. 変かわる is general transformation — something becomes different from what it was. 代かわる is substitution — one thing takes the place of another. 換かわる is exchange — swapping one for another. In speech they're identical. In writing, 変かわる covers the vast majority of situations.

The kanji 変へん is striking. The top part is a tangled mess — 亦 compressed and altered — over 夊 (slow, dragging feet). Something twisted, something off. The word carries that slightly unsettling quality: change isn't always welcome. 変かな人ひと (a weird person) uses the same kanji. Change and strangeness are neighbors.

変かわる is intransitive — change happens on its own. Its transitive partner is 変かえる (かえる, to change something). 天気てんきが変かわった (the weather changed) vs. 計画けいかくを変かえた (I changed the plan). The weather changes by itself. The plan needs someone to change it.

Particles: 変かわる takes が for the thing that changes: 景色いろが変かわる (the scenery changes). Use に for what it changes into: 赤あかに変かわる (turn red).

Passive に変かわられる: rare, since 変わる is already intransitive. Indirect passive only — someone else's change affects you. 担当とう者しゃに変かわられて困こまった (the person in charge changed on me and it caused problems).

Causative を／に変かわらせる: making something change. Uncommon — Japanese usually reaches for the transitive 変かえる instead. But it exists: 状じょう況を変かわらせる (make the situation change).

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 変える (かえる)

Polite negかわりませんが
Pastかわったが

て-formかわってが

meaning: first (of all), to begin with, for now
lesson:
まず does something subtle: it prioritizes. Not "first" as in "the first item on a list," but "first" as in "before anything else, let's handle this." まず食たべよう (let's eat first) doesn't mean eating is step one of a plan — it means eating is what matters right now, everything else can wait.

This makes まず a workhorse in conversation. Want to propose something without committing to a full plan? まずやってみよう (let's just try it first and see). Want to buy time? まず考かんがえさせてください (let me think about it first). The word creates breathing room — it acknowledges that more steps might follow without requiring you to know what they are yet.

There's also a secondary meaning that leans toward probability: まず大丈夫だいじょうぶだろう (it should be fine, most likely). まず間ま違いない (almost certainly not wrong). Here まず means "in all likelihood" — a hedged confidence. A different flavor from the "first" meaning, but both share the same instinct: setting a baseline before complications arrive.

社しゃ (community shrine) + 会かい (gathering). A community that gathers at the shrine. You've already seen both kanji in 会社かいしゃ (company) — same two characters, reversed. 会社かいしゃ is a specific organization. 社会しゃかい is the broader fabric: society, the social world, the system we all live in.

The word was actually coined in the Meiji era (late 1800s) when Japanese intellectuals needed a translation for the Western concept of "society." They combined these two kanji to build a word for something Japan didn't previously have a single term for — the abstract idea of a social collective beyond family and feudal structures. It's a manufactured compound, but it worked so well that it's now one of the most common nouns in Japanese.

社会人しゃかいじん (しゃかいじん) deserves special attention. Literally "society person," it means a working adult — someone who has graduated, entered the workforce, and become a functioning member of society. There's no clean English equivalent. Being a 社会あしゃかい人ひと carries expectations: you pay taxes, behave responsibly, know how to use keigo. 社会あしゃかいに出でる (to enter society — start working after school) is the transition point.

A false friend — sort of. プログラム comes from English "program" but its primary meaning in Japanese is more specific: the printed booklet or leaflet you get at a concert, play, ceremony, or school event listing the schedule and performers. プログラムを見みてください (please look at the program) almost always means the physical handout, not a TV show or computer software.

It can refer to the schedule or lineup of events itself: 今日きょうのプログラム (today's program / today's schedule of events). And in computing contexts, プログラム does mean a computer program — but you'll more often hear プログラミング for the act of programming. For TV programs, Japanese reaches for 番組ばんぐみ (ばんぐみ) instead.

The katakana spelling is long — ten characters — making it a good drill for reading katakana at speed. Sound it out: pu-ro-gu-ra-mu. The グ (gu) catches people who expect "gra" to be one syllable. In Japanese, every consonant needs its vowel.

The kanji 力ちから is a pictograph of a flexed arm — specifically, the tendons and muscles of a forearm straining. In the oldest bronze inscriptions, you can see the bicep bulging. Over millennia it simplified into two clean strokes, but the physical origin is still there. This is raw bodily strength made into a symbol.

As a standalone word, ちから covers physical strength, ability, and power broadly. 力ちからがある (to have strength / to be powerful). 力ちからを入いれる (to put in effort — literally, put in power). 全力ぜんりょく (ぜんりょく, full power). But in compounds, the reading shifts to りょく or りき: 能力のうりょく (のうりょく, ability), 努力どりょく (どりょく, effort), 力ちから士 (りきし, sumo wrestler).

力ちから as a radical appears in dozens of kanji, always adding a sense of force or exertion: 動うご (move), 助じょ (help), 勉 (study — effort applied), 勝か (win). Spot the little arm in the corner of these characters and you'll see the force at work.

今こん (now, this) + 回かい (time, round, turn). This particular occasion, this round, this go-around. It's more specific than just "now" — it implies a series of events and you're pointing at the current one. 今回こんかいは難むずかしかった (this time was hard) implies there were other times, and this one stood out.

The kanji 回かい is a spiral — a thing going around. Its counter usage (三さん回かい, three times) is everywhere, and 今回こんかい essentially means "the current revolution of the wheel." Pair it with its siblings: 前まえ回 (ぜんかい, last time), 次つぎ回 (じかい, next time), 毎回かいまいかい (まいかい, every time). These four show up in meetings, reviews, serialized content, games — anything that repeats.

今回こんかい is slightly more formal than 今いま度 (こんど, this time / next time), and unlike 今いま度 it never means "next time." If you say 今回こんかい, you're always talking about now, not later.

予よ (beforehand) + 定てい (fix, decide). Something decided in advance. A plan, a schedule, an expected arrangement. The word lives in the space between "intention" and "commitment" — firmer than つもり (personal intention), softer than a promise.

明日あしたの予定よていは？ (what are your plans for tomorrow?) is one of the most common questions in casual Japanese. 予定よていがある (I have plans) is the polite way to decline an invitation without specifying what those plans are. 予定よてい通り (よていどおり, as planned / on schedule) is what you want to hear from project managers.

Key pattern: verb (dictionary form) + 予定です = "I'm planning to / I'm scheduled to." 来月つきらいげつ引っ越す予定よていです (I'm planning to move next month). This is the standard way to talk about upcoming plans. It sounds natural and appropriately tentative — not a binding commitment, just what's on the calendar.

meaning: as is, still (in the current state)
lesson:
まま freezes things. Whatever state something is in, まま says it stays that way. そのまま (just like that, as it is). ドアを開あけたまま出でかけた (went out leaving the door open). 靴くつのまま入はいらないで (don't come in with your shoes still on).

The pattern is always the same: [state] + まま = "with that state unchanged." The state can be expressed with a past-tense verb (座すわったまま, still seated), an adjective (冷つめたいまま, still cold), or a noun + の (裸のまま, still naked). The grammar is flexible, but the meaning is constant: nothing has changed.

There's often an undertone of something being wrong or unresolved. 分わからないまま (still not understanding). 言いわれるまま (doing whatever you're told — passively staying in the state of being directed). まま can describe contentment (leaving things be), but just as often it describes a problem that persists because nobody addressed it.

Clipped from "television" — but only the first three syllables survived the journey into Japanese: te-re-bi. The "vision" half got dropped entirely. This kind of aggressive shortening is standard for katakana loanwords: パソコン (personal computer), リモコン (remote control), コンビニ (convenience store). Japanese borrows the word, then trims it down to something that fits comfortably in the mouth.

テレビを見みる (watch TV) is one of the first full sentences most learners construct, and it's worth noting that Japanese uses 見みる (see/look) rather than anything meaning "watch." You see television. You also see movies (映画えいがを見みる). The English distinction between "watch" and "see" doesn't map onto Japanese.

テレビをつける (turn on the TV) / テレビを消きす (turn off the TV). テレビに出でる (appear on TV) — the に marks the place where you show up. If you're talking about a specific program, that's 番ばん組 (ばんぐみ); テレビ refers to the medium or the physical device.

減へる is the intransitive half of a pair: things 減へる on their own (decrease), and you 減へらす (へらす) them on purpose. Population declines, savings shrink, patience wears thin — all 減へる. You cut expenses, reduce staff, lower the volume — all 減へらす.

The kanji 減げん has water (氵さんずい) on the left and 咸かん (which phonetically suggests cutting or diminishing) on the right. Water receding — like a river level dropping. お腹はらが減へった is the classic usage: "I'm hungry" — literally, my stomach has emptied. This is the casual, slightly rough way to say it (the politer version uses 空そらく). If a Japanese person says 腹はら減へった (はらへった), they're being very casual — it's a male-leaning, blunt expression of hunger.

Its opposite is 増ふえる (ふえる, to increase). Keep the pair together: 増ふえる / 減へる, up / down. They show up side by side in news, statistics, and any conversation about trends.

Particles: 減へる takes が — the thing that decreases is the subject. 人ひと口くちが減へる (the population decreases). 体重たいじゅうが減へった (I lost weight).

Passive に減へられる: indirect passive only — someone or something decreasing affects you negatively. スタッフに減へられると困こまる (if the staff decreases on me, I'm in trouble).

Causative を／に減へらせる: making something decrease. But Japanese typically uses the transitive 減へらす instead. The causative of 減る is grammatically valid but rarely encountered.

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 減らす (へらす)
Dictionaryへるが

Politeへりますが
Polite negへりませんが

て-formへってが

Polite volへりましょうが

Causative—

Imperative—
Imp なさい—
Cond ～ばへればが
Cond ～たらへったらが

消きえる is the sound of absence. A candle goes out. A stain fades. A person vanishes from your life. The word covers every kind of disappearing — physical, visual, metaphorical — as long as the thing was there and now it's not.

The kanji 消しょう is water (氵さんずい) plus 肖しょう (to resemble/diminish). Water washing something away until it diminishes to nothing. 火ひが消きえる (the fire goes out). 電気でんきが消きえた (the lights went out). 痛いたみが消きえる (the pain disappears). 記憶が消きえる (memories fade away).

This is intransitive — things vanish on their own. The transitive partner is 消けす (けす, to erase / turn off / extinguish), which you've already met. 消けす is you killing the light. 消きえる is the light dying by itself. Together they form one of the cleanest intransitive/transitive pairs in the language.

Particles: 消きえる takes が — the thing that disappears is the subject. 音おとが消きえた (the sound disappeared). Sometimes から marks the place it disappears from: 画が面めんから消きえた (disappeared from the screen).

Passive: rarely used, since the verb is already intransitive and describes a state rather than an action done by an agent.

Causative を消きえさせる: grammatically possible but unnatural. Use 消けす instead when you want the transitive meaning.

一段 Ichidan自動詞 — pair: 消す (けす)
Dictionaryきえるが
Negativeきえないが
Politeきえますが
Polite negきえませんが
Pastきえたが
Polite pastきえましたが
て-formきえてが
Volitionalきえようが

Cond ～ばきえればが
Cond ～たらきえたらが

家か (household) + 族ぞく (tribe, clan). Your household clan. 族ぞく is the interesting kanji here — it originally depicted a flag (方かた) over an arrow (矢), representing a military banner that a clan rallied under. The family as a unit marching under the same flag.

家族かぞく is the standard, neutral word for family. Not particularly formal, not particularly casual — just the word everyone uses. 家族かぞくは何人ひとですか (how many people in your family?). 家族かぞくと一緒に住すんでいる (I live with my family). It typically refers to the nuclear unit — the people living under your roof — though it can stretch to include extended family in context.

Japanese has several words for "family" at different registers: 家族かぞく is the default, 家いえ庭 (かてい) emphasizes the home environment, and 一いち家いえ (いっか) means the whole household/clan with a more dramatic ring to it. For "my family" in self-deprecating polite speech, you might hear 家いえのもの — but 家族かぞく is the one you'll use 90% of the time.

The kanji 比ひ is two people standing side by side — literally, two figures lined up for comparison. It's one of those rare kanji where the meaning and the shape match perfectly. Two things, next to each other, being measured against one another.

AとBを比くらべる is the standard frame: compare A with B. 去年きょねんと比くらべると暖あたたかい (compared to last year, it's warm). 兄あにと比くらべないでほしい (I don't want to be compared to my brother). The particle と marks what you're comparing against, while を marks the things being compared.

An extremely common set phrase: 〜に比べて — "compared to." 日にち本ほんに比くらべて物価が安い (compared to Japan, prices are cheap). This て-form usage shows up constantly in conversation and writing. It's one of those constructions where the grammar is technically a verb but it functions more like a preposition.

Particles: 比くらべる takes を for the things being compared: 二つふたつの案を比くらべる. Use と to mark the benchmark: 前まえ回と比くらべる. In the に比くらべて pattern, に marks the reference point.

Potential が比くらべられる: が — what can be compared becomes the subject. 簡単かんたんに比くらべられる (can be easily compared).

Passive に比くらべられる: に marks who does the comparing — or what you're compared to. いつも姉あねに比くらべられる (I'm always compared to my older sister).

Causative に…を比くらべさせる: に marks who you have compare, を marks what they compare. 学がく生うに二つの文を比くらべさせる (have students compare two sentences).

Politeくらべますを
Polite negくらべませんを
Pastくらべたを

Volitionalくらべようを

Potentialくらべられるが
Imperativeくらべろを
Imp なさいくらべなさいを
Cond ～ばくらべればを
Cond ～たらくらべたらを

meaning: to be born, come into existence
lesson:
生せい is one of the most versatile kanji in the language. It has at least ten different readings and appears in hundreds of compounds. As う（まれる） it means "to be born"; as い（きる）, "to live"; as なま, "raw"; as せい, "life" in compounds. All orbiting the same core idea: alive, growing, fresh.

生うまれる is intransitive — you are born; nobody "births" you in this construction. (The transitive partner 生うむ (うむ, to give birth) takes the mother as the subject.) 東京とうきょうで生うまれた (I was born in Tokyo). 新あたらしいアイデアが生うまれた (a new idea was born). The word extends naturally to anything that comes into existence — ideas, trends, technologies, works of art.

A useful conversational pattern: 生うまれも育ちも大おお阪 (born and raised in Osaka). 生うまれ (the noun form, "birth") pairs neatly with 育ち (upbringing) to locate someone's whole origin story in one phrase.

Particles: 生うまれる takes が for what's born: 子供こどもが生うまれた. Use で for where: 大おお阪で生うまれた. Use に for the time or family: 1990年としに生うまれた.

Passive: Already intransitive and semi-passive in meaning ("to be born"), so the passive form is essentially unused.

Causative を生うまれさせる: unnatural. Use 生うむ (to give birth to) or 生うみ出です (to create/produce) for the active meaning.

Something for nothing. ただでもらった (I got it for free). 入場にゅうじょうはただです (admission is free). The word is blunt and colloquial — the kind of thing you'd say to a friend, not write in a formal announcement. For that, you'd use 無料むりょう (むりょう).

But ただ has a second, completely unrelated meaning that shows up even more often: "just, merely, only." ただの友達ともだち (just a friend — nothing more). ただし (however — a formal conjunction). These aren't the same word historically, but they've collapsed into the same sound. Context separates them instantly: if money is involved, it means free. Otherwise, it means "merely."

There's a proverb: ただより高たかいものはない (nothing is more expensive than free). The Japanese instinct that free things come with hidden costs — social obligations, implied debts, strings attached. It's not cynicism so much as awareness of the unspoken exchange beneath every gift.

これ + ら = the plural. You know これ (this); ら is a suffix that loosely pluralizes. これらの問題もんだい (these problems). これらは全部ぜんぶ私わたしのです (these are all mine).

Japanese doesn't normally mark plurals, though. これ alone can already mean "this" or "these" depending on context. So when does これら earn its place? When you need to be explicitly clear that you're talking about multiple items, especially in writing. これら is more bookish than conversational — you'll see it in reports, academic writing, and formal speech more than in casual talk. In conversation, most people just say これ and gesture at the whole group.

The same ら suffix shows up in 彼かれら (かれら, they) and 私わたしたち (where たち serves the same pluralizing function). But ら and たち aren't quite interchangeable: たち is warmer, more inclusive. ら is cooler, more clinical. You'd never say これたち.

meaning: to investigate, examine, check, look into
lesson:
調しらべる is the verb for active information-seeking. Not casual browsing — deliberate checking. 辞書じしょで調しらべる (look it up in a dictionary). ネットで調しらべる (look it up online). 警察けいさつが事こと件を調しらべている (the police are investigating the case). It scales from "let me Google that" to "a team of detectives is on it."

The kanji 調ちょう has 言い (words) on the left and 周しゅう (around, thorough) on the right. Words going around thoroughly — examining from all sides. In compounds, the reading is usually ちょう: 調査ちょうさ (ちょうさ, investigation/survey), 調整ちょうせい (ちょうせい, adjustment).

調べる vs 探す: Both involve looking for something, but they're different searches. 探さがす (さがす) is searching for a physical thing or person — lost keys, a specific restaurant, a missing child. 調しらべる is searching for information or examining something for understanding. You 探さがす your phone. You 調しらべる why it stopped working.

Particles: 調しらべる takes を — the thing you're investigating is the direct object. 原因いんげんいんを調しらべる (investigate the cause). Use で for the tool or method: ネットで調しらべる.

Potential が調しらべられる: が — what can be investigated becomes the subject. スマホで簡単かんたんに調しらべられる (you can easily look it up on your phone).

Passive に調しらべられる: に marks who investigates. 警察けいさつに調しらべられた (I was investigated by the police).

Causative に…を調しらべさせる: に marks who you have investigate, を marks what. 部へ下したにデータを調しらべさせた (I had my subordinate check the data).

事じ (thing, matter) + 故こ (reason, cause, old). A "matter with a cause" — something that happened, and not for good reasons. The word leans negative: car crashes, workplace incidents, nuclear disasters. 交通事故こうつうじこ (こうつうじこ, traffic accident) is the compound you'll hear most.

故こ is a kanji worth knowing on its own. It means "old" or "the past" in some contexts (故郷ふるさと, こきょう, hometown) and "reason/cause" in others (故こに, ゆえに, therefore — very literary). In 事故じこ, it brings the sense of causation gone wrong — an event with an unfortunate cause.

事故じこに遭う (じこにあう, to meet with an accident) uses 遭そうう, the "encounter" verb reserved for bad luck. You don't 遭そうう good fortune — the verb itself tells you the encounter was unwelcome. 事故じこを起おこす (to cause an accident) is the other key pattern — 起おこす (to cause) puts the blame on the subject.

電でん (electricity) + 話わ (talk). Electric talk. The compound was coined when the telephone first arrived in Japan in the 1870s, and it's one of those translations that nails it: speech sent through electricity. Chinese, Korean, and Vietnamese all adopted versions of the same compound.

電話でんわ is a する-noun — slap する on it and it becomes a verb: 電話はなでんわする (to phone someone). 電話でんわをかける (to make a call — literally, hang/cast a call) is the slightly more specific phrasing. 電話でんわに出でる (to answer the phone — "come out to" the phone). 電話はなでんわを切きる (to hang up — "cut" the call).

In the smartphone age, 電話でんわ still specifically means a voice call. Texting is メール or メッセージ. So 電話はなでんわしようか (shall I call you?) is explicitly proposing voice over text — sometimes the more intimate or urgent choice. The word 携帯 (けいたい) or スマホ refers to the device; 電話でんわ refers to the act of calling or the concept of telephony.

外がい (outside) + 国こく (country). An outside country. The word is simple, but the worldview it encodes isn't: Japan is the default, and everything else is 外がい — outside. There's no neutral "other country" in daily Japanese. You're either in Japan or you're 外 of it.

This inside/outside framing runs deep. 外国人がいこくじん (がいこくじん, foreigner) is the standard, neutral term for a non-Japanese person. 外国語がいこくご (がいこくご, foreign language). 外国くにがいこくに行いく (go abroad). The word isn't rude — it's the default, polite term. But it does reveal how Japanese naturally categorizes: 内 (inside, us) versus 外 (outside, them).

外国 vs 海外: Both mean "abroad" but with different textures. 外国がいこく emphasizes the country (a specific foreign nation or foreign nations in general). 海外そとかいがい (かいがい, literally "across the sea") emphasizes the act of being overseas — travel, experience, distance. 外国くにがいこくの文化ぶんか (foreign culture) vs. 海外そとかいがいに住すむ (live overseas).

銀ぎん (silver) + 行こう (firm, business, row). A silver firm. You met 行こう in lesson 1 as いく (to go) — but here it's in its compound reading こう, carrying the older meaning of "a row of shops" or "a business establishment." The "silver" comes from the historical role of silver as currency in East Asian trade. Chinese banks were literally places that dealt in silver.

Modern 銀行ぎんこう is just a bank — ATMs, savings accounts, mortgages. 銀行いぎんこうに行く (go to the bank). 銀行いぎんこうに預ける (deposit at the bank). 銀行いぎんこうから下したろす (withdraw from the bank). Japan is still more cash-oriented than many countries, so 銀行ぎんこう and ATM come up in daily life frequently.

The kanji 銀ぎん has the metal radical 金きん on the left (which itself means gold — all metals get the gold radical) and 艮 on the right, which contributes the sound. 銀ぎん appears in 銀ぎん色いろ (ぎんいろ, silver color) and the famous 銀ぎん座すわ (ぎんざ, literally "silver seat" — the Tokyo shopping district, named after a historical silver mint).
